A paragraph (from the Ancient Greek παράγραφος, parágraphos, "to write beside")
is a self-contained unit of discourse in writing dealing with a particular point or idea.
A paragraph consists of one or more sentences. Though not required by the syntax of any language,
paragraphs are usually an expected part of formal writing, used to organize longer prose.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.text.line_of_text
documentation:
LineOfText represents a single line of text
LineOfText supports:
- font
- font_color
- font_size
- borders: border_top, border_right, border_bottom, border_left
- border_color
- border_width
- padding: padding_top, padding_right, padding_bottom, padding_left
- background_color
- horizontal_alignment
- vertical_alignment
text_alignment is not applicable for LineOfText, as its bounding box will always be the exact width needed
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory
documentation:
This class provides static convenience methods for generating points of common line-art in electronic documents,
such as arrows, rectangles, triangles, regular n-gons, stars, etc
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.EURion
documentation:
The EURion constellation (also known as Omron rings or doughnuts) is a pattern of symbols incorporated into a number of secure documents
such as banknotes and ownership title certificates designs worldwide since about 1996.
It is added to help imaging software detect the presence of such a document in a digital image.
Such software can then block the user from reproducing banknotes to prevent counterfeiting using colour photocopiers.
According to research from 2004, the EURion constellation is used for colour photocopiers but probably not used in computer software.
It has been reported that Adobe Photoshop will not allow editing of an image of a banknote,
but in some versions this is believed to be due to a different, unknown digital watermark rather than the EURion constellation.
:return:
CONSTRUCTOR
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_card
documentation:
This is the old IBM punched card. Each line of a program was punched into one IBM card.
Then the cards were stacked in order and taken to a card reader.
Usually the student would submit the cards and someone else would run them during the middle of the night,
when the computer wasn't so busy. The output was printed on wide z-fold paper.
If you made a mistake, you would have to resubmit the cards and wait another day.
Large programs had stacks of cards several feet high. If you are using this shape, you need to update your hardware.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_collate
documentation: Indicates a step that orders information into a standard format.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_data
documentation:
Indicates the process of inputting and outputting data,
as in entering data or displaying results. Represented as a rhomboid.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_database
documentation: Indicates a list of information with a standard structure that allows for searching and sorting.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_decision
documentation:
Shows a conditional operation that determines which one of the two paths the program will take.
The operation is commonly a yes/no question or true/false test. Represented as a diamond (rhombus).

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_delay
documentation:
Represents a segment of delay in a process.
It can be helpful to indicate the exact length of delay within the shape.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_direct_data
documentation:
Direct Data object in a process flow represents information stored which can be accessed directly.
This object represents a computer's hard drive.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_display
documentation: Indicates a step that displays information.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_document
documentation: Shows a printed document or report.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_extract
documentation:
The Extract shape involves removal of one or more specific sets of items from a set.
For example, you could have a list of addresses and extract those that are within 10 miles of some location.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_internal_storage
borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_loop_limit
documentation: Indicates the point at which a loop should stop.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_manual_input
documentation:
Represented by quadrilateral, with the top irregularly sloping up from left to right,
like the side view of a keyboard. Represents a step where a user is prompted to enter information manually.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_manual_operation
documentation:
Represented by a trapezoid with the longest parallel side at the top,
to represent an operation or adjustment to process that can only be made manually.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_merge
documentation: The Merge shape combines two or more sets of items into one set.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_multiple_documents
documentation: Represents multiple documents in a process

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_off_page_reference
documentation:
"
A labeled connector for use when the target is on another page.
Represented as a home plate-shaped pentagon.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_on_page_reference
documentation:
This small circle (also known as Connector) indicates that the next (or previous) step is somewhere else on the drawing.
This is particularly useful for large flowcharts where you would otherwise have to use a long connector,
which can be hard to follow.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_or
documentation: Just as described, this shape indicates that the process flow continues two paths or more.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_paper_tape
documentation:
An outdated symbol rarely ever used in modern practices or process flows,
but this shape could be used if you’re mapping out processes or input methods
on much older computers and CNC machines.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_predefined_document
documentation: Shows a predefined printed document or report.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_predefined_process
documentation:
Shows named process which is defined elsewhere.
Represented as a rectangle with double-struck vertical edges.[14]

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_preparation
documentation:
Represented by an elongated hexagon,
originally used for steps like setting a switch or initializing a routine.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_process
documentation: Represents a set of operations that changes value, form, or location of data. Represented as a rectangle.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_process_iso_9000
documentation: Represents a set of operations that changes value, form, or location of data. Represented as a rectangle.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_sequential_data
documentation:
This shape is supposed to look like a reel of tape with a small portion of tape extending from the reel.
It represents magnetic tape storage which is also called sequential access storage.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_sort
documentation: Indicates a step that organizes a list of items into a sequence or sets based on some pre-determined criteria.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_stored_data
documentation:
This is a general data storage object used in the process flow as opposed to data which could be also stored on a hard drive,
magnetic tape, memory card, of any other storage device.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_summing_junction
documentation: Indicates a point in the flowchart where multiple branches converge back into a single process.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_termination
documentation:
Indicates the beginning and ending of a program or sub-process.
Represented as a stadium, oval or rounded (fillet) rectangle.
They usually contain the word "Start" or "End", or another phrase signaling the start or end of a process,
such as "submit inquiry" or "receive product".

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.flowchart_transport
documentation: The Transport flowchart shape represents the step in the flowchart where information or materials are being transported from the process.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.four_pointed_star
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a four point star that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.fraction_of_circle
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a circle-slice that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.half_of_circle
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a circle-slice of 180 degrees that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.heart
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a heart that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.heptagon
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a heptagon that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.hexagon
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for a hexagon that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.isosceles_triangle
documentation: This function returns the coordinates for an isosceles triangle that fits in the given bounding box

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.line_art.line_art_factory.LineArtFactory.lissajours
documentation:
A Lissajous curve /ˈlɪsəʒuː/, also known as Lissajous figure or Bowditch curve /ˈbaʊdɪtʃ/, is the graph of a system of parametric equations
which describe complex harmonic motion.
This family of curves was investigated by Nathaniel Bowditch in 1815, and later in more detail in 1857 by Jules Antoine Lissajous (for whom it has been named).
The appearance of the figure is highly sensitive to the ratio x_frequency / y_frequency.
For a ratio of 1, the figure is an ellipse, with special cases including circles.
The visual form of these curves is often suggestive of a three-dimensional knot,
and indeed many kinds of knots, including those known as Lissajous knots, project to the plane as Lissajous figures.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.image.watermark
documentation:
A watermark is an identifying image or pattern in paper that appears as various shades of lightness/darkness when viewed
by transmitted light (or when viewed by reflected light, atop a dark background), caused by thickness or density variations in the paper.

Watermarks have been used on postage stamps, currency, and other government documents to discourage counterfeiting.
There are two main ways of producing watermarks in paper; the dandy roll process, and the more complex cylinder mould process.

Watermarks vary greatly in their visibility; while some are obvious on casual inspection,
others require some study to pick out. Various aids have been developed, such as watermark fluid that wets the paper without damaging it.

A watermark is very useful in the examination of paper because it can be used for dating documents and artworks,
identifying sizes, mill trademarks and locations, and determining the quality of a sheet of paper.

The word is also used for digital practices that share similarities with physical watermarks.
In one case, overprint on computer-printed output may be used to identify output from an unlicensed trial version of a program.
In another instance, identifying codes can be encoded as a digital watermark for a music, video, picture, or other file.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.image.watermark.Watermark

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.image.watermark.Watermark.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

documentation: PRIVATE

documentation:
This method paints this LayoutElement on the given Page, in the available space
\:param page: the Page on which to paint this LayoutElement
\:param available_space: the available space (as a Rectangle) on which to paint this LayoutElement
\:return: None
PUBLIC

A watermark is an identifying image or pattern in paper that appears as various shades of lightness/darkness when viewed
by transmitted light (or when viewed by reflected light, atop a dark background), caused by thickness or density variations in the paper.

Watermarks have been used on postage stamps, currency, and other government documents to discourage counterfeiting.
There are two main ways of producing watermarks in paper; the dandy roll process, and the more complex cylinder mould process.

Watermarks vary greatly in their visibility; while some are obvious on casual inspection,
others require some study to pick out. Various aids have been developed, such as watermark fluid that wets the paper without damaging it.

A watermark is very useful in the examination of paper because it can be used for dating documents and artworks,
identifying sizes, mill trademarks and locations, and determining the quality of a sheet of paper.

The word is also used for digital practices that share similarities with physical watermarks.
In one case, overprint on computer-printed output may be used to identify output from an unlicensed trial version of a program.
In another instance, identifying codes can be encoded as a digital watermark for a music, video, picture, or other file.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.circle_annotation

Square and circle annotations (PDF 1.3) shall display, respectively, a rectangle or an ellipse on the page. When
opened, they shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the associated note. The rectangle or ellipse
shall be inscribed within the annotation rectangle defined by the annotation dictionary’s Rect entry (see
Table 168).

shall be inscribed within the annotation rectangle defined by the annotation dictionary’s Rect entry (see
Table 168).

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.widget_annotation
documentation:
Interactive forms (see 12.7, “Interactive Forms”) use widget annotations (PDF 1.2) to represent the appearance
of fields and to manage user interactions. As a convenience, when a field has only a single associated widget
annotation, the contents of the field dictionary (12.7.3, “Field Dictionaries”) and the annotation dictionary may
be merged into a single dictionary containing entries that pertain to both a field and an annotation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.redact_annotation
documentation:
A redaction annotation (PDF 1.7) identifies content that is intended to be removed from the document. The
intent of redaction annotations is to enable the following process:

a) Content identification. A user applies redact annotations that specify the pieces or regions of content that
should be removed. Up until the next step is performed, the user can see, move and redefine these
annotations.

b) Content removal. The user instructs the viewer application to apply the redact annotations, after which the
content in the area specified by the redact annotations is removed. In the removed content’s place, some
marking appears to indicate the area has been redacted. Also, the redact annotations are removed from
the PDF document.

Redaction annotations provide a mechanism for the first step in the redaction process (content identification).
This allows content to be marked for redaction in a non-destructive way, thus enabling a review process for
evaluating potential redactions prior to removing the specified content.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.redact_annotation.RedactAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.redact_annotation.RedactAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A redaction annotation (PDF 1.7) identifies content that is intended to be removed from the document. The
intent of redaction annotations is to enable the following process:

a) Content identification. A user applies redact annotations that specify the pieces or regions of content that
should be removed. Up until the next step is performed, the user can see, move and redefine these
annotations.

b) Content removal. The user instructs the viewer application to apply the redact annotations, after which the
content in the area specified by the redact annotations is removed. In the removed content’s place, some
marking appears to indicate the area has been redacted. Also, the redact annotations are removed from
the PDF document.

Redaction annotations provide a mechanism for the first step in the redaction process (content identification).
This allows content to be marked for redaction in a non-destructive way, thus enabling a review process for
evaluating potential redactions prior to removing the specified content.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.sound_annotation
documentation:
A sound annotation (PDF 1.2) shall analogous to a text annotation except that instead of a text note, it contains
sound recorded from the computer’s microphone or imported from a file. When the annotation is activated, the
sound shall be played. The annotation shall behave like a text annotation in most ways, with a different icon (by
default, a speaker) to indicate that it represents a sound. Table 185 shows the annotation dictionary entries
specific to this type of annotation. Sound objects are discussed in 13.3, “Sounds.”
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

sound shall be played. The annotation shall behave like a text annotation in most ways, with a different icon (by
default, a speaker) to indicate that it represents a sound. Table 185 shows the annotation dictionary entries
specific to this type of annotation. Sound objects are discussed in 13.3, “Sounds.”

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.free_text_annotation
documentation:
A free text annotation (PDF 1.3) displays text directly on the page. Unlike an ordinary text annotation (see
12.5.6.4, “Text Annotations”), a free text annotation has no open or closed state; instead of being displayed in a
pop-up window, the text shall be always visible. Table 174 shows the annotation dictionary entries specific to
this type of annotation. 12.7.3.3, “Variable Text” describes the process of using these entries to generate the
appearance of the text in these annotations.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

this type of annotation. 12.7.3.3, “Variable Text” describes the process of using these entries to generate the

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.text_annotation
documentation:
A text annotation represents a “sticky note” attached to a point in the PDF document. When closed, the
annotation shall appear as an icon; when open, it shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the note
in a font and size chosen by the conforming reader. Text annotations shall not scale and rotate with the page;
they shall behave as if the NoZoom and NoRotate annotation flags (see Table 165) were always set. Table 172
shows the annotation dictionary entries specific to this type of annotation.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.text_annotation.TextAnnotationIconType
documentation: This Enum represents all possible text annotation icon types

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.text_annotation.TextAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.text_annotation.TextAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A text annotation represents a “sticky note” attached to a point in the PDF document. When closed, the
annotation shall appear as an icon; when open, it shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the note
in a font and size chosen by the conforming reader. Text annotations shall not scale and rotate with the page;
they shall behave as if the NoZoom and NoRotate annotation flags (see Table 165) were always set. Table 172
shows the annotation dictionary entries specific to this type of annotation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.popup_annotation
documentation:
A pop-up annotation (PDF 1.3) displays text in a pop-up window for entry and editing. It shall not appear alone
but is associated with a markup annotation, its parent annotation, and shall be used for editing the parent’s text.
It shall have no appearance stream or associated actions of its own and shall be identified by the Popup entry
in the parent’s annotation dictionary (see Table 174). Table 183 shows the annotation dictionary entries specific
to this type of annotation.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.popup_annotation.PopupAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.popup_annotation.PopupAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A pop-up annotation (PDF 1.3) displays text in a pop-up window for entry and editing. It shall not appear alone
but is associated with a markup annotation, its parent annotation, and shall be used for editing the parent’s text.
It shall have no appearance stream or associated actions of its own and shall be identified by the Popup entry
in the parent’s annotation dictionary (see Table 174). Table 183 shows the annotation dictionary entries specific
to this type of annotation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.trap_net_annotation
documentation:
A trap network annotation (PDF 1.3) may be used to define the trapping characteristics for a page of a PDF
document.

-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

A trap network annotation (PDF 1.3) may be used to define the trapping characteristics for a page of a PDF
document.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.rubber_stamp_annotation
documentation:
A rubber stamp annotation (PDF 1.3) displays text or graphics intended to look as if they were stamped on the
page with a rubber stamp. When opened, it shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the associated
note. Table 181 shows the annotation dictionary entries specific to this type of annotation.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

documentation: This Enum represents all possible rubber stamp annotation icons

documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A rubber stamp annotation (PDF 1.3) displays text or graphics intended to look as if they were stamped on the
page with a rubber stamp. When opened, it shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the associated
note. Table 181 shows the annotation dictionary entries specific to this type of annotation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.remote_go_to_annotation
documentation:
A link annotation represents either a hypertext link to a destination elsewhere in the document (see 12.3.2,
“Destinations”) or an action to be performed (12.6, “Actions”). Table 173 shows the annotation dictionary
entries specific to this type of annotation.
This method adds a link annotation with an action that opens a remote URI.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.remote_go_to_annotation.RemoteGoToAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.remote_go_to_annotation.RemoteGoToAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A link annotation represents either a hypertext link to a destination elsewhere in the document (see 12.3.2,
“Destinations”) or an action to be performed (12.6, “Actions”). Table 173 shows the annotation dictionary
entries specific to this type of annotation.
This method adds a link annotation with an action that opens a remote URI.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.printer_mark_annotation
documentation:
A printer’s mark annotation (PDF 1.4) represents a graphic symbol, such as a registration target, colour bar, or
cut mark, that may be added to a page to assist production personnel in identifying components of a multiple-
plate job and maintaining consistent output during production. See 14.11.3, “Printer’s Marks,” for further
discussion.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.printer_mark_annotation.PrinterMarkAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.printer_mark_annotation.PrinterMarkAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A printer’s mark annotation (PDF 1.4) represents a graphic symbol, such as a registration target, colour bar, or
cut mark, that may be added to a page to assist production personnel in identifying components of a multiple-
plate job and maintaining consistent output during production. See 14.11.3, “Printer’s Marks,” for further
discussion.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.polyline_annotation
documentation:
Polygon annotations (PDF 1.5) display closed polygons on the page. Such polygons may have any number of
vertices connected by straight lines. Polyline annotations (PDF 1.5) are similar to polygons, except that the first
and last vertex are not implicitly connected.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.polyline_annotation.LineEndStyleType
documentation: This Enum represents all possible line end styles

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.polyline_annotation.PolylineAnnotation

documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

Polygon annotations (PDF 1.5) display closed polygons on the page. Such polygons may have any number of
vertices connected by straight lines. Polyline annotations (PDF 1.5) are similar to polygons, except that the first
and last vertex are not implicitly connected.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.three_d_annotation
documentation:
3D annotations (PDF 1.6) are the means by which 3D artwork shall be represented in a PDF document.
Table 298 shows the entries specific to a 3D annotation dictionary. Table 164 describes the entries common to
all annotation dictionaries.

In addition to these entries, a 3D annotation shall provide an appearance stream in its AP entry (see Table 164)
that has a normal appearance (the N entry in Table 168). This appearance may be used by applications that do
not support 3D annotations and by all applications for the initial display of the annotation.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.three_d_annotation.ThreeDAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.three_d_annotation.ThreeDAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

3D annotations (PDF 1.6) are the means by which 3D artwork shall be represented in a PDF document.
Table 298 shows the entries specific to a 3D annotation dictionary. Table 164 describes the entries common to
all annotation dictionaries.

In addition to these entries, a 3D annotation shall provide an appearance stream in its AP entry (see Table 164)
that has a normal appearance (the N entry in Table 168). This appearance may be used by applications that do
not support 3D annotations and by all applications for the initial display of the annotation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.annotation
documentation:
An annotation associates an object such as a note, sound, or movie with a location on a page of a PDF
document, or provides a way to interact with the user by means of the mouse and keyboard. PDF includes a
wide variety of standard annotation types, described in detail in 12.5.6, “Annotation Types.”
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.annotation.Annotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.annotation.Annotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.annotation.Annotation._timestamp_to_str
documentation: PRIVATE

An annotation associates an object such as a note, sound, or movie with a location on a page of a PDF
document, or provides a way to interact with the user by means of the mouse and keyboard. PDF includes a
wide variety of standard annotation types, described in detail in 12.5.6, “Annotation Types.”

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.watermark_annotation
documentation:
A watermark annotation (PDF 1.6) shall be used to represent graphics that shall be printed at a fixed size and
position on a page, regardless of the dimensions of the printed page. The FixedPrint entry of a watermark
annotation dictionary (see Table 190) shall be a dictionary that contains values for specifying the size and
position of the annotation (see Table 191).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.watermark_annotation.WatermarkAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.watermark_annotation.WatermarkAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A watermark annotation (PDF 1.6) shall be used to represent graphics that shall be printed at a fixed size and
position on a page, regardless of the dimensions of the printed page. The FixedPrint entry of a watermark
annotation dictionary (see Table 190) shall be a dictionary that contains values for specifying the size and
position of the annotation (see Table 191).

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.file_attachment_annotation
documentation:
A file attachment annotation (PDF 1.3) contains a reference to a file, which typically shall be embedded in the
PDF file (see 7.11.4, “Embedded File Streams”).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.file_attachment_annotation.FileAttachmentAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.file_attachment_annotation.FileAttachmentAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

A file attachment annotation (PDF 1.3) contains a reference to a file, which typically shall be embedded in the
PDF file (see 7.11.4, “Embedded File Streams”).

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.square_annotation
documentation:
Square and circle annotations (PDF 1.3) shall display, respectively, a rectangle or an ellipse on the page. When
opened, they shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the associated note. The rectangle or ellipse
shall be inscribed within the annotation rectangle defined by the annotation dictionary’s Rect entry (see
Table 168).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.square_annotation.SquareAnnotation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.square_annotation.SquareAnnotation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

Square and circle annotations (PDF 1.3) shall display, respectively, a rectangle or an ellipse on the page. When
opened, they shall display a pop-up window containing the text of the associated note. The rectangle or ellipse
shall be inscribed within the annotation rectangle defined by the annotation dictionary’s Rect entry (see
Table 168).

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.annotation.polygon_annotion
documentation:
Polygon annotations (PDF 1.5) display closed polygons on the page. Such polygons may have any number of
vertices connected by straight lines. Polyline annotations (PDF 1.5) are similar to polygons, except that the first

and last vertex are not implicitly connected.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art
documentation:
A SmartArt graphic is a visual representation of your information and ideas.
You create one by choosing a layout that fits your message.
Some layouts (such as organization charts and Venn diagrams) portray specific kinds of information,
while others simply enhance the appearance of a bulleted list.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.alternating_picture_list
documentation:
Use to show a series of pictures from top to bottom. Text appears alternately on the right or left of the picture.
\:param texts: the typing.List[str] to be used as
\:param pictures the typing.List[str] to be used as image URLs
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement
CONSTRUCTOR
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.ascending_block_list
documentation:
Use to show groups of related ideas or lists of information.
The text shapes increase in height sequentially, and the Level 1 text displays vertically.
\:param text_level_1: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param text_level_2 the typing.List\[typing.List[str]\] to be used as level 2 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.basic_bending_process
documentation:
Use to show a long or non-linear sequence or steps in a task, process, or workflow.
Works best with Level 1 text only. Maximizes both horizontal and vertical display space for shapes.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.closed_chevron_process
documentation:
Use to show a progression, a timeline, or sequential steps in a task, process, or workflow, or to emphasize movement or direction.
Can be used to emphasize information in the starting shape. Works best with Level 1 text only.
\:param text:
\:param font_size:
\:param foreground_color:
\:param background_color:
\:param font_color:
\:return:

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.descending_block_list
documentation:
Use to show groups of related ideas or lists of information.
The text shapes decrease in height sequentially, and the Level 1 text displays vertically.
\:param text_level_1: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param text_level_2 the typing.List\[typing.List[str]\] to be used as level 2 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.horizontal_bullet_list
documentation:
A horizontal bullet list is used to show non-sequential information or grouped data, yet it will not affect the outcome of the other data.
The first block on the left is related to the second block as well as the third block but the process of the
second block is not dependent on how the process on the first block is implemented.
Apart from this, each block of information has the same emphasis, and it does not connote any direction.
\:param text_level_1: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param text_level_2 the typing.List\[typing.List[str]\] to be used as level 2 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.horizontal_process
documentation:
Use to show a progression or sequential steps in a task, process, or workflow.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.matrix
documentation:
A matrix is a rectangular array of elements, arranged in rows and columns, and can be used to show the placement of concepts along two axes.
For example, you can use a matrix to illustrate the four possible combinations of two concepts or ingredients.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used in the 4 boxes
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.opposing_ideas
documentation:
Use to show two opposing or contrasting ideas. Has two Level 1 items.
Works well with large amounts of text.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.picture_list
documentation:
Use to show a series of pictures from top to bottom. Text appears on the right of the picture.
\:param texts: the typing.List[str] to be used as
\:param pictures the typing.List[str] to be used as image URLs
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.table_hierarcy
documentation:
Use to show groups of information built from top to bottom, and the hierarchies within each group.
This layout does not contain connecting lines.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.tags
documentation:
---
param background_color: the background_color of the tags
param font_color: the font_color of the tags
param font_size: the font_size of the tags
param foreground_color: the foreground_color (unused)
param s: the typing.List[str] to be converted to tags
return: true
---

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.vertical_bullet_list
documentation:
A vertical bullet list is used to show non-sequential information or grouped data, yet it will not affect the outcome of the other data.
The first block on the top is related to the second block as well as the third block but the process of the
second block is not dependent on how the process on the first block is implemented.
Apart from this, each block of information has the same emphasis, and it does not connote any direction.
\:param text_level_1: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param text_level_2 the typing.List\[typing.List[str]\] to be used as level 2 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.smart_art.smart_art.SmartArt.vertical_process
documentation:
Use to show a progression or sequential steps in a task, process, or workflow.
\:param text: the typing.List[str] to be used as level 1 text
\:param font_size: the font_size to be used
\:param foreground_color: the Color to be used in the foreground
\:param background_color: the color to be used in the background
\:param font_color: the Color to be used for text
\:return: a LayoutElement

A SmartArt graphic is a visual representation of your information and ideas.
You create one by choosing a layout that fits your message.
Some layouts (such as organization charts and Venn diagrams) portray specific kinds of information,
while others simply enhance the appearance of a bulleted list.
fmt: on

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.geography.map_of_the_world
documentation:
A world map is a map of most or all of the surface of Earth. World maps, because of their scale,
must deal with the problem of projection.
Maps rendered in two dimensions by necessity distort the display of the three-dimensional surface of the Earth.
While this is true of any map, these distortions reach extremes in a world map.
Many techniques have been developed to present world maps that address diverse technical and aesthetic goals.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

must deal with the problem of projection.
Maps rendered in two dimensions by necessity distort the display of the three-dimensional surface of the Earth.
While this is true of any map, these distortions reach extremes in a world map.
Many techniques have been developed to present world maps that address diverse technical and aesthetic goals.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.geography.map
documentation:
A world map is a map of most or all of the surface of Earth. World maps, because of their scale,
must deal with the problem of projection.
Maps rendered in two dimensions by necessity distort the display of the three-dimensional surface of the Earth.
While this is true of any map, these distortions reach extremes in a world map.
Many techniques have been developed to present world maps that address diverse technical and aesthetic goals.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.hyphenation.hyphenation
documentation:
Syllabification or syllabication, also known as hyphenation, is the separation of a word into syllables, whether spoken, written or signed.
A hyphenation algorithm is a set of rules, especially one codified for implementation in a computer program,
that decides at which points a word can be broken over two lines with a hyphen.
For example, a hyphenation algorithm might decide that impeachment can be broken as impeach-ment or im-peachment but not impe-achment.
One of the reasons for the complexity of the rules of word-breaking is that different "dialects" of English tend to differ on hyphenation[citation needed]:
American English tends to work on sound,
but British English tends to look to the origins of the word and then to sound.
There are also a large number of exceptions, which further complicates matters.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.hyphenation.hyphenation.Hyphenation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.hyphenation.hyphenation.Hyphenation.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.hyphenation.hyphenation.Hyphenation.hyphenate
documentation:
This function hyphenates the input word, inserting the hyphenation_character wherever the word *can* be split
in syllables. This function returns the word with hyphenation_character inserted.
\:param s: the str to be hyphenated
\:param hyphenation_character: the character to insert to mark hyphenation
\:return: the hyphenated str
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

Syllabification or syllabication, also known as hyphenation, is the separation of a word into syllables, whether spoken, written or signed.
A hyphenation algorithm is a set of rules, especially one codified for implementation in a computer program,
that decides at which points a word can be broken over two lines with a hyphen.
For example, a hyphenation algorithm might decide that impeachment can be broken as impeach-ment or im-peachment but not impe-achment.
One of the reasons for the complexity of the rules of word-breaking is that different "dialects" of English tend to differ on hyphenation[citation needed]:
American English tends to work on sound,
but British English tends to look to the origins of the word and then to sound.
There are also a large number of exceptions, which further complicates matters.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.font.adobe_glyph_list
documentation:
The Adobe Glyph List (AGL) is a mapping of 4,281 glyph names to one or more Unicode characters.
Its purpose is to provide an implementation guideline for consumers of fonts (mainly software applications);
it lists a variety of standard names that are given to glyphs that correspond to certain Unicode character sequences.
The AGL is maintained by Adobe Systems.
For producers of fonts, Adobe suggests a more limited set of names, the Adobe Glyph List for New Fonts (AGLFN),
based on an earlier version of the AGL.
Names not in the AGLFN are to be constructed by standard heuristics described in Unicode and Glyph Names.
AGL and AGLFN, along with related resources, are currently maintained and available at the AGL & AGLFN open source project.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.font.adobe_glyph_list.AdobeGlyphList

The Adobe Glyph List (AGL) is a mapping of 4,281 glyph names to one or more Unicode characters.
Its purpose is to provide an implementation guideline for consumers of fonts (mainly software applications);
it lists a variety of standard names that are given to glyphs that correspond to certain Unicode character sequences.
The AGL is maintained by Adobe Systems.
For producers of fonts, Adobe suggests a more limited set of names, the Adobe Glyph List for New Fonts (AGLFN),
based on an earlier version of the AGL.
Names not in the AGLFN are to be constructed by standard heuristics described in Unicode and Glyph Names.
AGL and AGLFN, along with related resources, are currently maintained and available at the AGL & AGLFN open source project.

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.page_layout.PageLayout.get_page
documentation:
This function returns the Page for which this PageLayout is active.
:return:    the Page

This class acts as a layoutmanager for `Document` objects.
Instead of adding `LayoutElements` to a `Page` by calling `layout` (and needing to know the precise coordinates)
you can simply use this class and its `add` method.
Any implementation of `PageLayout` should keep track of where `LayoutElement` objects are placed,
and what the remaining free space is.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout
documentation:
This module contains various implementations of `PageLayout`.
`PageLayout` can be used to add `LayoutElement` objects to a `Page` without
having to specify coordinates.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout.__init__

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout._add_header_and_footer
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout.add
documentation:
This method adds a `LayoutElement` to the current `Page`.
The specific implementation of `PageLayout` should decide where the `LayoutElement` will be placed.
\:param layout_element: the LayoutElement to be added
\:return: self
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout.switch_to_next_column
documentation:
This function forces this PageLayout to move to the next column on the Page
:return:    self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.multi_column_layout.MultiColumnLayout.switch_to_next_page
documentation:
This function forces this PageLayout to move to the next Page
:return:    self

This implementation of PageLayout adds left/right/top/bottom margins to a Page
and lays out the content on the Page as if there were multiple  columns to flow text, images, etc into.
Once a column is full, the next column is automatically selected, although the next column can be manually selected.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.inline_flow
documentation:
This implementation of LayoutElement aggregates other LayoutElements
and lays them out on consecutive lines. If a line is full, it overflows
into the next line.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

\:return: self

This implementation of LayoutElement aggregates other LayoutElements
and lays them out on consecutive lines. If a line is full, it overflows
into the next line.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.page_layout.single_column_layout_with_overflow

This implementation of PageLayout adds left/right/top/bottom margins to a Page
and lays out the content on the Page as if there were was a single column to flow text, images, etc into.
Once this column is full, the next page is automatically created.

This implementation of PageLayout adds left/right/top/bottom margins to a Page

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table_util
documentation: This class contains utility methods for using the Table classes in borb.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table_util.TableUtil.from_2d_array
documentation:
This function creates a Table from a 2D array of (stringable) data
\:param data: the data used to populate the Table
\:param background_color: the background color of cells in the Table
\:param flexible_column_width: true if a FlexibleColumnWidthTable should be used, false otherwise
\:param font_color: the font-color of cells in the Table
\:param font_size: the font-size of cells in the Table
\:param header_background_color: the background color of header cells in the Table
\:param header_col: whether there is a header column
\:param header_font_color: the font-color of header cells in the Table
\:param header_row: whether there is a header row
\:param round_to_n_digits: this value is None if digits should not be rounded, if this value is not None, digits are rounded to this precision
\:return: a Table containing the data
CONSTRUCTOR
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table_util.TableUtil.from_pandas_dataframe
documentation:
This function creates a Table from a 2D array of a pandas.DataFrame
\:param data: the data used to populate the Table
\:param background_color: the background color of cells in the Table
\:param flexible_column_width: true if a FlexibleColumnWidthTable should be used, false otherwise
\:param font_color: the font-color of cells in the Table
\:param font_size: the font-size of cells in the Table
\:param header_background_color: the background color of header cells in the Table
\:param header_col: whether there is a header column
\:param header_font_color: the font-color of header cells in the Table
\:param header_row: whether there is a header row
\:param round_to_n_digits: this value is None if digits should not be rounded, if this value is not None, digits are rounded to this precision
\:return: a Table containing the data
This class contains utility methods for using the Table classes in borb.
fmt: on

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder
documentation: This implementation of EventListener keeps track of which space on a Page is available
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder._event_occurred
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.find_free_space_for_page
documentation:
This function returns the nearest (Euclidean distance)
empty Rectangle that is at least as wide and tall as the
desired Rectangle.
If no such Rectangle exists, this method returns None.
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.get_free_space_for_page
documentation:
This function returns the nearest (euclidean distance)
empty Rectangle that is at least as wide and tall as the
desired Rectangle.
If no such Rectangle exists, this method returns None.

inner_classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.Grid

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.Grid.get_free_space
documentation: This function returns a Rectangle (or None) of free space (no text rendering operations, no drawing operations) near the given Rectangle

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.free_space_finder.FreeSpaceFinder.Grid.mark_as_unavailable
documentation:
This method marks a given area in this Grid as unavailable for future content
\:param rectangle: the Rectangle to be marked
\:return: self

This class represents a rasterized, low-res view of a Page.
But rather than rendering instructions on this raster, each cell simply keeps track
of whether the cell is available. This enables a quick lookup for availability of a given Rectangle.
This implementation of EventListener keeps track of which space on a Page is available

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table
documentation:
This class represents a common base for all LayoutElement implementations
that attempt to represent tabular data.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell._get_content_box
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_column_span
documentation:
This function returns the column span. This specifies the number of columns a cell should span.
:return:    The column span
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_layout_box
documentation:
This function returns the previous result of layout
:return:    the Rectangle that was the result of the previous layout operation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_layout_element
documentation:
This function returns the LayoutElement in this TableCell
:return:    the LayoutElement inside this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_max_width
documentation:
This function returns the maximum width this TableCell (determined by the LayoutElement inside it).
This function returns None if the maximum width has not yet been determined.
:return:    the maximum width of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_max_height
documentation:
This function returns the maximum height this TableCell (determined by the LayoutElement inside it).
This function returns None if the maximum height has not yet been determined.
:return:    the maximum height of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_min_width
documentation:
This function returns the minimum width this TableCell (determined by the LayoutElement inside it).
This function returns None if the minimum width has not yet been determined.
:return:    the minimum width of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_min_height
documentation:
This function returns the minimum height this TableCell (determined by the LayoutElement inside it).
This function returns None if the minimum height has not yet been determined.
:return:    the minimum height of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_preferred_height
documentation:
This function returns the preferred height of this TableCell (determined by a previous run of the LayoutElement framework).
This function returns None if the preferred height has not yet been determined.
:return:    the preferred height of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_preferred_width
documentation:
This function returns the preferred width of this TableCell (determined by a previous run of the LayoutElement framework).
This function returns None if the preferred width has not yet been determined.
:return:    the preferred width of this TableCell

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_previous_layout_box
documentation:
This function returns the previous result of layout of this LayoutElement
:return:    the Rectangle that was the result of the previous layout operation

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_row_span
documentation:
This function returns the row span. This specifies the number of rows a cell should span.
:return:    The row span

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.TableCell.get_table_coordinates
documentation:
This function returns the coordinates (in the Table) of this TableCell.
These coordinates may be a single typing.Tuple[int, int] or a typing.List of such
tuples in case of row/column span.
:return:    the table coordinates
This class represents a single cell of a table

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table._get_cells_at
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.add
documentation:
This function adds the given LayoutElement to this Table.
This function returns self.
\:param layout_element: the LayoutElement to be added
\:return: self
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.even_odd_row_colors
documentation:
This function colors the Table with the classic "zebra stripes"
e.a. one color for all even rows, and a contrasting color for the odd rows.
This function returns self.
\:param even_row_color: the Color to be used for even rows
\:param odd_row_color: the Color to be used for odd rows
\:param header_row_color: the Color to be used for the header row, if None is specified the even_row_color will be used
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.get_cells_at_column
documentation:
This function returns all TableCell elements at a given column
\:param column: the specified column
\:return: all TableCell elements at the given column

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.get_cells_at_row
documentation:
This function returns all TableCell elements at a given row
\:param row: the specified row
\:return: all TableCell elements at the given row

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.get_number_of_columns
documentation:
This function returns the number of columns in this Table
:return:    the number of columns

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.get_number_of_rows
documentation:
This function returns the number of rows in this Table
:return:    the number of rows

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.internal_borders
documentation:
This method sets the border(s) on all TableCell objects in this Table
except for the borders that form the outside edge of the Table
:return:    self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.no_borders
documentation:
This method unsets the border(s) on all TableCell objects in this Table
:return:    self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.outer_borders
documentation:
This method unsets the border(s) on all TableCell objects in this Table
except for the borders that form the outside edge of the Table
:return:    self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.outer_borders_rounded
documentation:
This function sets rounded borders on the outside of this Table
\:param border_radius: the desired border radius
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.set_background_color_on_all_cells
documentation:
This method sets the background Color on all TableCell objects in this Table
\:param background_color: the background Color
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.set_border_color_on_all_cells
documentation:
This method sets the border color on all TableCell objects in this Table
\:param border_color: the border Color
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.set_border_width_on_all_cells
documentation:
This method sets the border width on all TableCell objects in this Table
\:param border_width: the border width
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.set_borders_on_all_cells
documentation:
This method sets the border(s) on all TableCell objects in this Table
\:param border_top: True if a top border should be drawn, False otherwise
\:param border_right: True if a right border should be drawn, False otherwise
\:param border_bottom: True if a bottom border should be drawn, False otherwise
\:param border_left: True if a left border should be drawn, False otherwise
\:return: self

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.table.table.Table.set_padding_on_all_cells
documentation:
This method sets the padding on all TableCell objects in this Table
\:param padding_top: the top padding
\:param padding_right: the right padding
\:param padding_bottom: the bottom padding
\:param padding_left: the left padding
\:return: self

This class represents a common base for all LayoutElement implementations
that attempt to represent tabular data.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element
documentation:
This file contains all the classes needed to perform layout.
This includes an Alignment Enum type, and the base implementation of LayoutElement
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.Alignment
documentation:
In typesetting and page layout, alignment or range is the setting of text flow or image placement relative to a page,
column (measure), table cell, or tab.
The type alignment setting is sometimes referred to as text alignment,
text justification, or type justification.
The edge of a page or column is known as a margin, and a gap between columns is known as a gutter.

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement._get_border_outline
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement._needs_to_be_tagged
documentation:
This function returns whether this LayoutElement needs to be tagged
\:param p: the Page on which this LayoutElement is to be painted
\:return: true if this LayoutElement needs to be tagged, False otherwise

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement.get_font_size
documentation: This function returns the font size of this LayoutElement
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.layout.layout_element.LayoutElement.get_golden_ratio_landscape_box
documentation:
This function returns the layout box that fits this LayoutElement
and whose ratio of dimensions (width / height) are closest to the golden ratio.
:return:    the layout box (in landscape mode) with ratio closest to the golden ratio

-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

documentation: This function returns the y-coordinate of the lower-left of this Rectangle

documentation:
This function returns a (slightly) larger Rectangle,
grown by the given amount

documentation:
This function returns True if this Rectangle intersects with the given Rectangle,
False otherwise

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.rectangle.Rectangle.shrink
documentation:
This function returns a (slightly) smaller Rectangle,
shrunk by the given amount

In Euclidean plane geometry, a rectangle is a quadrilateral with four right angles.
It can also be defined as an equiangular quadrilateral, since equiangular means that all of its angles are equal (360°/4 = 90°).
It can also be defined as a parallelogram containing a right angle. A rectangle with four sides of equal length is a square.
The term oblong is occasionally used to refer to a non-square rectangle.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix
documentation:
In mathematics, a matrix (plural matrices) is a rectangular array or table of numbers,
symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.__init__
documentation: Initialize a new Matrix
CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.__deepcopy__
documentation: PRIVATE

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.cross
documentation:
This method calculates the dot-product of this Matrix
with an input vector (represented by 3 input Decimal objects)
and returns the result
\:param x: the first component of the vector
\:param y: the second component of the vector
\:param z: the third component of the vector
\:return: the result vector
PUBLIC

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.determinant
documentation:
In linear algebra, the determinant is a scalar value that can be computed from the elements of a square matrix
and encodes certain properties of the linear transformation described by the matrix.
The determinant of a matrix A is denoted det(A), det A, or |A|.
Geometrically, it can be viewed as the volume scaling factor of the linear transformation described by the matrix.
This is also the signed volume of the n-dimensional parallelepiped spanned by the column or row vectors of the matrix.
The determinant is positive or negative according to whether the linear transformation preserves or reverses the orientation of a real vector space.
:return:    the determinant of this Matrix

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.identity_matrix
documentation:
The identity matrix In of size n is the n-by-n matrix in which all the elements on the main diagonal
are equal to 1 and all other elements are equal to 0.
:return:    the identity Matrix

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.matrix_from_six_values
documentation:
This method returns the matrix \[[a, b, 0], [c, d, 0], [e, f, 1]\]
\:param a: the component at (0, 0)
\:param b: the component at (0, 1)
\:param c: the component at (1, 0)
\:param d: the component at (1, 1)
\:param e: the component at (2, 0)
\:param f: the component at (2, 1)
\:return: the Matrix

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.geometry.matrix.Matrix.mul
documentation:
This function multiplies this Matrix with another Matrix, returning the result
\:param y: the Matrix to multiply by
\:return: the resulting Matrix

In mathematics, a matrix (plural matrices) is a rectangular array or table of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns.
Provided that they have the same size (each matrix has the same number of rows and the same number of columns as the other),
two matrices can be added or subtracted element by element (see conformable matrix).

The rule for matrix multiplication, however, is that two matrices can be multiplied only when the number of columns
in the first equals the number of rows in the second (i.e., the inner dimensions are the same, n for an (m×n)-matrix times an (n×p)-matrix,
resulting in an (m×p)-matrix). There is no product the other way round—a first hint that matrix multiplication is not commutative.
Any matrix can be multiplied element-wise by a scalar from its associated field.

Lorem Ipsum is simply dummy text of the printing and typesetting industry.
Lorem Ipsum has been the industry's standard dummy text ever since the 1500s,
when an unknown printer took a galley of type and scrambled it to make a type specimen book.
It has survived not only five centuries, but also the leap into electronic typesetting, remaining essentially unchanged.
It was popularised in the 1960s with the release of Letraset sheets containing Lorem Ipsum passages,
and more recently with desktop publishing software like Aldus PageMaker including versions of Lorem Ipsum.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.move_to_next_line
documentation:
Move to the start of the next line. This operator has the same effect as the
code
0 -Tl Td
where Tl denotes the current leading parameter in the text state. The
negative of Tl is used here because Tl is the text leading expressed as a
positive number. Going to the next line entails decreasing the
y coordinate.

the name of a font resource in the Font subdictionary of the current
resource dictionary; size shall be a number representing a scale factor.
There is no initial value for either font or size; they shall be specified
explicitly by using Tf before any text is shown.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.set_text_matrix
documentation:
Set the text matrix, Tm , and the text line matrix, Tlm :
Tm = Tlm = [[a,b,0], [c,d,0],[e,f,1]]
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.set_text_matrix.SetTextMatrix

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.set_text_matrix.SetTextMatrix.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.set_text_matrix.SetTextMatrix.invoke
documentation:
Invoke the Tm operator
\:param canvas_stream_processor: the CanvasStreamProcessor
\:param operands: the operands for this CanvasOperator
\:param event_listeners: the typing.List of EventListener(s) that may be notified
\:return: None
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

Set the text matrix, Tm , and the text line matrix, Tlm :
Tm = Tlm = [[a,b,0], [c,d,0],[e,f,1]]

The operands shall all be numbers, and the initial value for Tm and Tlm
shall be the identity matrix, [ 1 0 0 1 0 0 ]. Although the operands
specify a matrix, they shall be passed to Tm as six separate numbers, not
as an array.

The matrix specified by the operands shall not be concatenated onto the
current text matrix, but shall replace it.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.show_text_with_glyph_positioning
documentation:
Show one or more text strings, allowing individual glyph positioning. Each
element of array shall be either a string or a number.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.show_text_with_glyph_positioning.ShowTextWithGlyphPositioning

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.show_text_with_glyph_positioning.ShowTextWithGlyphPositioning.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.text.show_text_with_glyph_positioning.ShowTextWithGlyphPositioning.invoke
documentation:
Invoke the TJ operator
\:param canvas_stream_processor: the CanvasStreamProcessor
\:param operands: the operands for this CanvasOperator
\:param event_listeners: the typing.List of EventListener(s) that may be notified
\:return: None
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

Show one or more text strings, allowing individual glyph positioning. Each
element of array shall be either a string or a number. If the element is a
string, this operator shall show the string. If it is a number, the operator
shall adjust the text position by that amount; that is, it shall translate the
text matrix, T . The number shall be expressed in thousandths of a unit
mof text space (see 9.4.4, "Text Space Details"). This amount shall be
subtracted from the current horizontal or vertical coordinate, depending
on the writing mode. In the default coordinate system, a positive
adjustment has the effect of moving the next glyph painted either to the
left or down by the given amount. Figure 46 shows an example of the
effect of passing offsets to TJ.

An operator in a programming language is a symbol that tells the compiler or interpreter to perform specific mathematical,
relational or logical operation and produce final result.
CanvasOperator defines an interface to work on Canvas objects. Typically these operators involve drawing graphics, text,
setting the active color and so on

Paint the specified XObject. The operand name shall appear as a key in

"PostScript XObjects").

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.path_construction.begin_subpath
documentation:
Begin a new subpath by moving the current point to
coordinates (x, y), omitting any connecting line segment. If
the previous path construction operator in the current path
was also m, the new m overrides it; no vestige of the
previous m operation remains in the path.

Begin a new subpath by moving the current point to

previous m operation remains in the path.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.path_construction.close_subpath
documentation:
Close the current subpath by appending a straight line
segment from the current point to the starting point of the
subpath. If the current subpath is already closed, h shall do
nothing.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.path_construction.close_subpath.CloseSubpath

methods.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.path_construction.close_subpath.CloseSubpath.__init__
documentation: CONSTRUCTOR

documentation:
Invoke the h operator
\:param canvas_stream_processor: the CanvasStreamProcessor
\:param operands: the operands for this CanvasOperator
\:param event_listeners: the typing.List of EventListener(s) that may be notified
\:return: None
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

Close the current subpath by appending a straight line
segment from the current point to the starting point of the
subpath. If the current subpath is already closed, h shall do
nothing.

This operator terminates the current subpath. Appending
another segment to the current path shall begin a new
subpath, even if the new segment begins at the endpoint
reached by the h operation.

modules.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.color.set_color_space_stroking
documentation:
(PDF 1.1) Set the current colour space to use for stroking operations. The
operand name shall be a name object. If the colour space is one that can
be specified by a name and no additional parameters (DeviceGray,
DeviceRGB, DeviceCMYK, and certain cases of Pattern), the name may
be specified directly. Otherwise, it shall be a name defined in the
ColorSpace subdictionary of the current resource dictionary (see 7.8.3,
"Resource Dictionaries"); the associated value shall be an array
describing the colour space (see 8.6.3, "Colour Space Families").

The names DeviceGray, DeviceRGB, DeviceCMYK, and Pattern
always identify the corresponding colour spaces directly; they never refer
to resources in the ColorSpace subdictionary.

The CS operator shall also set the current stroking colour to its initial
value, which depends on the colour space:
In a DeviceGray, DeviceRGB, CalGray, or CalRGB colour space, the
initial colour shall have all components equal to 0.0.
In a DeviceCMYK colour space, the initial colour shall be
[ 0.0 0.0 0.0 1.0 ].

In a Lab or ICCBased colour space, the initial colour shall have all
components equal to 0.0 unless that falls outside the intervals specified
by the space’s Range entry, in which case the nearest valid value shall be
substituted.

In an Indexed colour space, the initial colour value shall be 0.
In a Separation or DeviceN colour space, the initial tint value shall be 1.0
for all colorants.
In a Pattern colour space, the initial colour shall be a pattern object that
causes nothing to be painted.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: borb.pdf.canvas.operator.color.set_color_space_stroking.SetColorSpaceStroking

\:param operands: the operands for this CanvasOperator
\:param event_listeners: the typing.List of EventListener(s) that may be notified
\:return: None
PRIVATE
PUBLIC

(PDF 1.1) Set the current colour space to use for stroking operations. The
operand name shall be a name object. If the colour space is one that can
be specified by a name and no additional parameters (DeviceGray,
DeviceRGB, DeviceCMYK, and certain cases of Pattern), the name may
be specified directly. Otherwise, it shall be a name defined in the
ColorSpace subdictionary of the current resource dictionary (see 7.8.3,
"Resource Dictionaries"); the associated value shall be an array
describing the colour space (see 8.6.3, "Colour Space Families").

The names DeviceGray, DeviceRGB, DeviceCMYK, and Pattern
always identify the corresponding colour spaces directly; they never refer
to resources in the ColorSpace subdictionary.

The CS operator shall also set the current stroking colour to its initial
value, which depends on the colour space:
In a DeviceGray, DeviceRGB, CalGray, or CalRGB colour space, the
initial colour shall have all components equal to 0.0.
In a DeviceCMYK colour space, the initial colour shall be
[ 0.0 0.0 0.0 1.0 ].

In a Lab or ICCBased colour space, the initial colour shall have all
components equal to 0.0 unless that falls outside the intervals specified
by the space’s Range entry, in which case the nearest valid value shall be
substituted.

In an Indexed colour space, the initial colour value shall be 0.
In a Separation or DeviceN colour space, the initial tint value shall be 1.0
for all colorants. In a Pattern colour space, the initial colour shall be a pattern object that
causes nothing to be painted.

colour space; see 8.6.5.6, "Default Colour Spaces") and set the colour to
use for stroking operations. Each operand shall be a number between 0.0
(zero concentration) and 1.0 (maximum concentration). The behaviour of
this operator is affected by the overprint mode (see 8.6.7, "Overprint
Control").
